Of course, please provide the paragraph you would like me to rewrite, and I will ensure that all LaTeX commands stay intact, and no new abbreviations are introduced.

We consider a cell-free mMIMO system with $K$ single-antenna UEs and $L$ APs as shown in Fig. \ref{fig-sys}. 
The APs are arbitrarily located in the coverage area. Each AP equips with $N$ antennas. The total number of antennas in the coverage area is $M=NL$, where $M\gg K$. 
All APs are connected to the CPU through the fronthaul for cooperation. 
The APs simultaneously serve the UEs in the coverage area via joint coherent transmission and reception.
We consider the standard block flat fading channel between the APs and the UEs. 
Let $\mathbf{h}_{kl}\in \mathbb{C}^{N\times 1}$ be the channel vector between AP $l$ and UE $k$.
The channel vector $\mathbf{h}_{kl}$ follows a correlated Rayleigh fading distribution, i.e., $\mathbf{h}_{kl}\sim \mathcal{N}_{\mathbb{C}}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_{kl})$, where $\mathbf{R}_{kl}$ is the spatial correlation matrix describing the large-scale fading, including pathloss, shadowing, and spatial channel correlation 
between user $k$ and AP $l$. 
 We assume that $\mathbf{R}_{kl}$ is available at the APs since it can be obtained using practical correlation matrix estimation methods \cite{8338075}. 
The channel vectors of different APs are assumed to be independently distributed since all APs are separately located in the coverage area. The channel vector $\mathbf{h}_{kl}$ stays constant during each coherence block with a length of $\tau_c$. The system adopts the standard time division duplex (TDD) protocol in cell-free massive MIMO, in which each coherence block is divided into $\tau_p$ time-slots for UL pilot, $\tau_u$ time-slots for UL data, and $\tau_d$ times-slots for DL data, where $\tau_p+\tau_u+\tau_d=\tau_c$.

In this study, we investigate a cell-free massive MIMO (mMIMO) environment comprising $L$ Access Points (APs) and $K$ User Equipments (UEs) each with a single antenna, corresponding to the configuration depicted in Figure \ref{fig-sys}. The APs are distributed in an arbitrary manner over the service area, and every AP is equipped with $N$ antennas. The aggregate antenna count for the entire service territory is denoted by $M=NL$, with the condition that $M$ is substantially greater than $K$. A centralized processing unit (CPU) is connected to all APs via fronthaul connections to facilitate cooperative operations. Joint coherent processing for transmission and reception tasks is employed by the APs to concurrently serve all the UEs within their service domain.

The interaction between APs and UEs is characterized by a standard block flat fading channel model. For AP $l$ and UE $k$, the channel is represented by $\mathbf{h}_{kl}\in \mathbb{C}^{N\times 1}$. This channel vector $\mathbf{h}_{kl}$ is subject to a correlated Rayleigh fading pattern, which can be expressed as $\mathbf{h}_{kl}\sim \mathcal{N}_{\mathbb{C}}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_{kl})$. Here, $\mathbf{R}_{kl}$ symbolizes the spatial correlation matrix and encapsulates the large-scale fading factors such as path loss, shadowing effects, and the spatial correlations of the channel between the $k$th UE and $l$th AP.

It is postulated that all APs are privy to $\mathbf{R}_{kl}$, as these spatial correlation matrices can be reliably estimated using established techniques, as per the reference cited in \cite{8338075}. Additionally, the channel vectors corresponding to different APs are considered to be independent due to the dispersed positioning of the APs within the service area. The matrix $\mathbf{h}_{kl}$ is presumed to remain invariant through the duration of a coherence block, which spans $\tau_c$ intervals. Adhering to the typical time division duplex (TDD) protocol found in cell-free mMIMO systems, each coherence block is segmented into $\tau_p$ slots allocated for uplink (UL) pilot signals, $\tau_u$ slots for UL data transmission, and $\tau_d$ slots for downlink (DL) data communication, satisfying the equation $\tau_p+\tau_u+\tau_d=\tau_c$.
Reightly fading

I apologize for any confusion. The channel model described in your original text does not include "Reightly fading" but rather uses "Rayleigh fading ," which is a standard statistical model for the effect of a propagation environment on a radio signal, such as that found in wireless communication.

So, the original text correctly mentions correlated Rayleigh fading distribution: $\mathbf{h}_{kl}\sim \mathcal{N}_{\mathbb{C}}(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}_{kl})$, where $\mathbf{R}_{kl}$ is the spatial correlation matrix. If this was an error in the provided instructions, please clarify, and I will ensure the text reflects the correct terminology.

Cell-free mMIMO has drawn much attention due to its capability of providing wireless data transmission with high spectral efficiency (SE) and uniformly good services in a designated area \cite{8379438}. In contrast to conventional cellular systems, a cell-free network is not partitioned into cells. The user equipment (UE) in the network 
is simultaneously served by multiple nearby access points (APs) \cite{8768014}. The APs equipped with single or multiple antennas distributively locate in the coverage area and each of them is connected to a central processing unit (CPU) via fronthaul links so that they can share data or channel state information (CSI) for coordinated transmission or reception. The distributed nature of cell-free mMIMO enables large micro diversity gains to combat the random pathloss of wireless channels \cite{8761828}. 
The cooperation among APs allows coherent joint transmission or reception to suppress interference among UEs. Due to the above reasons, cell-free mMIMO has been seen as one of the promising physical layer techniques for beyond 5G networks in the future \cite{9356519}.

The concept of cell-free massive MIMO (mMIMO) is rapidly gaining traction for its proficiency in delivering high spectral efficiency (SE) and consistently superior service quality across a specified region \cite{8379438}. Unbound by the traditional cell structure of cellular networks, a cell-free system enables multiple access points (APs) to serve user equipment (UE) simultaneously \cite{8768014}. These APs, whether outfitted with single or multiple antennas, are interspersed throughout the coverage area and are each tethered to a central processing unit (CPU) through fronthaul connections. This setup facilitates the exchange of data and channel state information (CSI), fostering coordinated operations in both transmission and reception. The inherent distributed architecture of cell-free mMIMO proffers substantial microdiversity advantages, which are instrumental in counteracting the inherently unpredictable pathloss characteristic of wireless channels \cite{8761828}. Inter-AP cooperation catalyzes coherent joint signal handling, curtailing interference across UEs effectively. Given these attributes, cell-free mMIMO is often earmarked as a key candidate for the physical layer architectures that will shape beyond-5G networks \cite{9356519}.

In recent years, many existing works have studied uplink (UL) detection or downlink (DL) precoding techniques for cell-free mMIMO \cite{9586055}. For DL precoding, the cooperation among APs can be mainly performed on two levels: fully distributed \cite{9529197,9043895,8886730,8815488} or fully centralized   \cite{7917284,9064545}. 
In fully centralized precoding, the CPU preprocesses the DL data signal based on network-wide instantaneous CSI, so that the interference among UEs can be effectively suppressed. The authors in \cite{7917284} investigated the achievable SE and power control optimization of centralized zero-forcing (ZF) precoding. The optimal max-min fairness (MMF) power allocation method for centralized ZF precoding is designed by maximizing the minimum SE of all UEs iteratively. A low-complexity heuristic scheme is also proposed to achieve the near-optimal performance of MMF power allocation.   
The authors in \cite{9403922} designed centralized minimum mean squared error (MMSE) precoding and corresponding power control policy for DL data transmission. The results revealed that centralized MMSE can improve DL data rates over centralized ZF under uniform or MMF power allocation. Although centralized precoding provides high SE via network-wide interference suppression, it requires the APs to send their locally estimated instantaneous CSI to the CPU for facilitating the precoding design, which leads to enormous burdens on fronthaul links due to the CSI overhead.

In the landscape of research for cell-free massive MIMO (mMIMO) systems, a significant number of studies have been devoted to optimizing uplink (UL) detection and downlink (DL) precoding approaches \cite{9586055}. The DL precoding collaboration among APs can occur in two primary modes: either in a fully distributed manner \cite{9529197,9043895,8886730,8815488} or through a fully centralized approach \cite{7917284,9064545}. 

With centralized DL precoding, the central processing unit (CPU) manipulates the DL data signals according to the collective instantaneous channel state information (CSI) from the entire network. This centralized processing is key to effectively mitigating interference among UEs. Research detailed in \cite{7917284} delves into the attainable spectral efficiency (SE) and explores power control optimization strategies for centralized zero-forcing (ZF) precoding. Notably, they developed an optimal power allocation method to achieve maximum-minimum fairness (MMF), where the smallest SE among all UEs is maximized through iterative adjustments. Additionally, a heuristic algorithm with reduced complexity was introduced to approach the performance of the optimal MMF allocation closely.   

Further research reported in \cite{9403922} presented the design of centralized minimum mean squared error (MMSE) precoding along with an associated power control strategy tailored for DL transmissions. The findings indicate that centralized MMSE has the potential to outperform centralized ZF in enhancing DL data rates, whether under a uniform or MMF power allocation strategy. Although centralized precoding is advantageous for high SE through global interference reduction, it necessitates the relay of locally estimated instantaneous CSI from the APs to the CPU. This leads to significant fronthaul overhead, placing considerable strain on the fronthaul connections as a result of the CSI transfer requirements.

For UL signal detection, the cooperation among APs can be performed on three levels: fully centralized, fully distributed, and semi-distributed LSFD \cite{8845768}. In fully centralized combining \cite{8886730,8730536,9064545}, each AP forwards its received signal to the CPU without any local processing, while the CPU conducts the channel estimation and signal combining for UL decoding. Due to the network-wide instantaneous CSI available at the CPU, centralized combining can effectively suppress interference among UEs. However, it requires instantaneous CSI exchange between the APs and CPU, which leads to heavy CSI overhead in fronthaul links. This motivates the design of distributed combining methods for UL signal detection. In a fully distributed setup, each AP estimates its local CSI without sending it to the CPU. Instead, each AP combines its received signal based on local CSI and sends its local estimate of the UL signal to the CPU. CPU generates an overall estimate of the UL signal by simply averaging the corresponding local estimates sent by APs without using any CSI.
The authors in \cite{7827017} utilized maximum ratio (MR) for UL signal combining and derived the corresponding closed-form spectral efficiency (SE). The results showed that cell-free mMIMO with simple MR combining and proper power control can significantly improve the $95\%$-likely SE when compared with conventional small-cell systems. More sophisticated distributed combining solutions are proposed to improve the UL performance over MR. The authors in \cite{9529197} designed full-pilot zero forcing (FZF) combining to locally cancel the interference among pilot-sharing UEs. FZF requires the amount of antenna at the APs to be larger than the number of mutually orthogonal pilots. To alleviate this constraint, they proposed local partial zero forcing (LPZF), in which the AP only cancel the co-pilot interference from strong UEs. Later, the authors in \cite{9064545} proposed local MMSE (LMMSE) to allow local interference suppression at the APs with arbitrary number of antenna. 
Due to the lack network-wide CSI at APs, distributed combining can not fully suppress the interference among UE, thus its performance is far worse than that of centralized combining. Because the APs cannot fully suppress the interference among UEs merely based on their local CSI. 
To close the performance gap between distributed and centralized combining, LSFD was proposed to improve the performance of distributed combining. LSFD was first designed in UL multi-cell mMIMO systems for pilot contamination suppression in the name of pilot contamination decoding \cite{6875290,7837706}. Later, it was introduced into UL cell-free mMIMO to improve the achievable SE of distributed combining by leveraging large-scale fading (LSF) coefficients of the channel for interference suppression \cite{8585131,7869024,9174860,9739189}. LSFD is a semi-distributed combining scheme with two-layer decoding. The first layer adopts distributed combiners at APs based on local instantaneous CSI. The second layer is performed at the CPU via LSFD weighting based on network-wide statistical CSI. LSFD weights for different UEs are optimized independently by maximizing the corresponding signal-to-noise-plus-interference ratio (SINR) of each UE.
The authors in \cite{7869024} proposed LSFD with local MR combining (LSFD-MR) and derived the closed-form SE expression. The results showed that LSFD-MR achieves two-fold gains over MR receiver in terms of $5\%$-outage rate. The authors in \cite{9529197} designed an LSFD receiver with FZF and LPZF as the local combiners and derived the closed-form expression of achievable UL SE. The authors in \cite{8845768} analyzed the fronthaul signaling and achievable SE of three different cooperation levels for UL signal combining, where LSFD with LMMSE combiner (LSFD-LMMSE) was designed as the second level of cooperation among APs. Overall, compared with distributed combining solutions, LSFD significantly improves the UL SE via interference suppression at the CPU based on statistical CSI. Meanwhile, the fronthaul signaling overhead caused by CSI exchange between the APs and CPU in LSFD is much lower than centralized MMSE/ZF combining. As a result, LSFD provides an important alternative solution that allows a trade-off between UL data rates and system scalability/CSI overhead.

Recent studies have delved into various cooperative approaches for uplink (UL) signal detection in cell-free massive MIMO (mMIMO) systems, aligning them into three categories: fully centralized, fully distributed, and semi-distributed large-scale fading decoding (LSFD) \cite{8845768}. Centralized combining methods \cite{8886730,8730536,9064545} entail APs relaying their received signals to the CPU directly, with no preprocessing. The CPU then handles channel estimation and signal combining for UL decoding. Centralized combining is distinguished by its ability to mitigate UE interference using network-wide instantaneous channel state information (CSI). Nevertheless, transmitting instantaneous CSI from APs to the CPU imposes substantial overhead on fronthaul links, prompting interest in distributed combining strategies for UL detection.

Within a fully distributed framework, each AP independently estimates local CSI and computes received signal combinations accordingly, forwarding only the local UL signal estimations to the CPU. The CPU then computes an aggregate signal estimate by averaging the local estimates from the APs, devoid of any reliance on CSI.

Notably, \cite{7827017} showed that employing maximum ratio (MR) combining for UL detection and applying proper power control considerably enhances the 95th percentile spectral efficiency (SE) compared with conventional small-cell networks. To surpass the UL performance achieved with MR combining, more advanced distributed methods have been developed. With the introduction of full-pilot zero forcing (FZF) \cite{9529197}, APs can locally eliminate interference from UEs sharing the same pilots, contingent on the APs having more antennas than the available orthogonal pilots. When this requirement becomes a limiting factor, local partial zero forcing (LPZF) offers a solution by having APs only cancel interference from the strongest co-pilot UEs. Building on this, local minimum mean squared error (LMMSE) combining \cite{9064545} further refines interference suppression at APs without restrictions on antenna count.

Despite these advances, distributed combining is less effective in interference mitigation due to the absence of network-wide CSI at the APs. Therefore, its SE performance generally falls short of that achieved by centralized methods.

To bridge this gap, LSFD was introduced, originally devised for multi-cell mMIMO to counteract pilot contamination \cite{6875290,7837706} and subsequently adapted for cell-free mMIMO to enhance the SE of distributed combining by using large-scale fading coefficients \cite{8585131,7869024,9174860,9739189}. As a semi-distributed strategy, LSFD features a two-tiered decoding process: distributed combining at APs using local instantaneous CSI, followed by LSFD weighting at the CPU based on network-wide statistical CSI.

In \cite{7869024}, LSFD combined with local MR (LSFD-MR) was explored, yielding a closed-form SE expression and demonstrating SE improvement over MR alone. Delving into various local combiners, \cite{9529197} proposed an LSFD receiver utilizing FZF and LPZF and quantified the achievable UL SE. Additionally, \cite{8845768} scrutinized the fronthaul signaling and SE implications across the spectrum of cooperation levels, specifically detailing LSFD with an LMMSE combiner (LSFD-LMMSE) as a mid-tier cooperative approach.

In summary, LSFD significantly boosts UL SE through CPU-based interference mitigation employing statistical CSI, while concurrently reducing the fronthaul overhead associated with CSI exchange in comparison to centralized MMSE/ZF methods. Consequently, LSFD emerges as a middle ground, providing a compromise between UL data rates and manageability of CSI overhead/system scalability.

However, compared with UL detection, the corresponding DL LSFP in cell free mMIMO for interference suppression based on LSF coefficients of the channel has not been well studied in the literature.
Actually, DL LSFP problem is much more challenging than UL LSFD. Because LSFP weights of each UE affect the DL SINR of others as shown in \cite{9053886}, while the LSFD weights of each UE are independent of the UL SINR of others.   
In \cite{6283031,6736528,8408515}, the authors designed a two-layer LSFP method for multi-cell mMIMO systems. Leveraging the closed-form SINR expression of MR precoding and channel orthogonality under an infinity number of antennas at APs (denoted as $N$), LSFP weights for different UEs can be computed independently by suppressing the pilot contamination via ZF criteria, known as ZF-LSFP. Recently, the authors in \cite{9880647} introduced  ZF-LSFP into cell-free mMIMO systems for pilot contamination reduction. Simulation results in \cite{6736528,8408515} showed that ZF-LSFP only gives performance improvement in systems with very large $N$. To address this problem, a max-min optimization was utilized in \cite{8408515} to find the optimal LSFP weights in multi-cell mMIMO systems with small or average
values of $N$, where LSFP weights of all UEs are jointly optimized. A capacity lower bound of optimal LSFP was derived for multi-cell 
mMIMO systems with a finite value of $N$. The results demonstrated that LSFP leads to the effective elimination of inter-cell interference under local MR precoding at base stations. In \cite{9053886}, the authors also consider the LSFP design with local MR precoding in multi-cell mMIMO, while LSFP weights of the UEs are jointly optimized by maximizing the product of SINRs of all UEs. Moreover, the method in \cite{9053886,8408515} can only apply to systems with local MR precoding under spatially uncorrelated channels.

While considerable research has been directed at uplink (UL) detection techniques in cell-free massive MIMO (mMIMO) networks, exploring the potential of large-scale fading decoding (LSFD), the study of the downlink (DL) counterpart, known as large-scale fading precoding (LSFP), remains relatively underexplored, particularly concerning interference suppression through channel large-scale fading coefficients.

The challenges inherent in DL LSFP are more profound than those in UL LSFD. In DL, the LSFP weights assigned for one UE can influence the SINR of all other UEs, creating a complex interdependency that complicates the precoding process \cite{9053886}. This stands in contrast to UL LSFD, where the weights for each UE could be optimized independently without affecting other UEs' SINRs.

Previous works such as those found in \cite{6283031,6736528,8408515} have proposed a two-tier LSFP framework for multicell mMIMO configurations. By leveraging the closed-form expression for the SINR with maximum ratio (MR) precoding and assuming an arbitrarily large number of antennas at the APs (symbolized as $N$), these methods were able to compute LSFP weights for individual UEs autonomously. The approach, known as zero-forcing LSFP (ZF-LSFP), aimed to mitigate pilot contamination. However, as shown in \cite{9880647}, introducing ZF-LSFP into cell-free mMIMO systems primarily benefits scenarios with exceedingly large $N$.

To extend the applicability of LSFP to systems with modest or average antenna counts, \cite{8408515} employed a max-min fairness optimization to calculate optimal LSFP weights – a joint optimization over all UEs. This optimization provided a lower bound on the network capacity for multicell mMIMO systems, even with a finite $N$. The findings illustrate that strategic LSFP can substantially mitigate inter-cell interference, particularly when coupled with local MR precoding at the base stations.

The research in \cite{9053886} also investigated LSFP design in multicell MIMO systems, adopting local MR precoding and collectively optimizing LSFP weights by maximizing SINR products across UEs. Nevertheless, the methods discussed in \cite{9053886,8408515} were constrained by the prerequisite of spatially uncorrelated channels and the use of local MR precoding.

This distinction underscores the pressing need for further exploration of DL LSFP solutions in cell-free mMIMO, which could foster methodologies capable of handling the complex interdependencies of DL SINRs and extend the benefits of LSFP to more diverse network configurations and realistic channel conditions.

The authors in \cite{10113887} introduce a virtual-LSFP (V-LSFP) with more general local precoding methods (such as MR, L-MMSE), drawing from the perspective of UL-DL duality, where normalized local combining and LSFD vectors obtained in UL transmission are repurposed for DL precoding. Furthermore, energy-efficient LSFP schemes are designed by optimizing the sparsity of V-LSFP vectors.

In the quest to refine downlink (DL) precoding for cell-free massive MIMO (mMIMO) systems, the study presented in \cite{10113887} introduces the concept of virtual-LSFP (V-LSFP). The novel framework expands upon the conventional large-scale fading precoding (LSFP) by incorporating a wider array of local precoding techniques, such as maximum ratio (MR) and local minimum mean squared error (L-MMSE), thereby departing from the traditional reliance on maximum ratio combiners.

This innovative approach to V-LSFP leans on the principle of uplink-downlink (UL-DL) duality. It translates the UL-normalized local combining vectors and LSFD vectors gleaned from UL transmissions for use in DL precoding. By harnessing the vectors determined in the UL phase, V-LSFP seamlessly adapts them for the DL phase, maintaining efficient system performance while minimizing the additional overhead typically associated with computing separate precoding vectors for DL.

Furthermore, the innovation doesn't stop at the mere application of UL-DL duality. The authors extend their exploration into the realm of energy efficiency, proposing LSFP schemes that prioritize the sparsity of the V-LSFP vectors. By optimizing for sparsity, these schemes aim to curtail the computational complexity and power consumption involved in the implementation of LSFP, positioning V-LSFP as a potentially more practical and environmentally sustainable alternative, especially pertinent for future large-scale implementations where energy efficiency will be pivotal.

The approach taken in \cite{10113887} underscores a significant move towards more practical and versatile implementations of LSFP in cell-free mMIMO networks, setting a foundation for future research to build upon in the pursuit of optimizing network performance while also addressing the pivotal issue of energy conservation.
rewrite the following sentences. Keep it short

In \cite{10113887}, the authors propose a virtual-LSFP (V-LSFP) method that employs diverse local precoding strategies like MR and L-MMSE for cell-free mMIMO systems, using UL-DL duality to repurpose UL vectors for DL precoding. They also optimize V-LSFP for energy efficiency by reducing vector complexity.

To address the aforementioned issue, we formulate an alternative problem to find optimal LSFP vectors for the UEs independently. In the alternative optimization problem, the objective is to find optimal $\mathbf{b}_k$ that maximizes the useful signal of UE $k$ while minimizing its interference to others. To this end, we define the \emph{signal-to-interference-to-others ratio} (SIOR) of UE $k$ as follows

To mitigate the complexity associated with interdependent LSFP vectors affecting multiple UEs, we propose an optimization problem tailored to derive optimal LSFP vectors for UEs individually. The new optimization aims to enhance UE $k$'s signal while reducing its interference impact on other UEs. We introduce the concept of the \emph{signal-to-interference-to-others ratio} (SIOR) for UE $k$ to achieve this goal.

Numerical simulation results are used to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed LSFP methods. The APs and UEs are independently and uniformly deployed in a $1\times 1$km squared field. We adopt the same propagation model in \cite{9064545} with spatially correlated fading. The APs are assumed to be deployed in urban environments with roughly 10m above the ground. The simple uniform power control scenario is considered for UL pilot and data transmission, in which all UE transmit with the same power $\eta_k=p_k=100$mW for $k\in \{1,\cdots, K\}$. The maximum DL transmission power of each AP is set to be $\rho_d=1000$mW. The channel bandwidth is 20MHz. The length of the coherence block is assumed to be $\tau_c=200$. Each block is divided into $\tau_p=10$ for the UL pilot, $\tau_u=100$ for UL data, and $\tau_d=90$ for DL data. As the numbers of APs, antennas per AP, and UEs in the network, we consider the following two cases unless otherwise stated: $L=100$, $N=1$, $K=25$ for Case 1; $L=100$, $N=4$, $K=50$ for Case 2.

The proposed LSFP approaches are validated using numerical simulations. APs and UEs are uniformly distributed across a $1\times 1$ km square area. The same spatially correlated fading propagation model from \cite{9064545} is applied, with APs situated roughly 10m high in an urban setting. For UL pilot and data phases, a simple uniform power control is assumed, with each UE transmitting at a power level of $\eta_k=p_k=100$ mW, where $k$ ranges from 1 to $K$. The peak DL power per AP is $\rho_d=1000$ mW, and the channel bandwidth is 20 MHz. The coherence time block is set to $\tau_c=200$, divided into $\tau_p=10$ for UL pilots, $\tau_u=100$ for UL data, and $\tau_d=90$ for DL data transmission. Unless specified otherwise, we consider two scenarios: Case 1 with $L=100$ APs, $N=1$ antenna per AP, and $K=25$ UEs; Case 2 with $L=100$ APs, $N=4$ antennas per AP, and $K=50$ UEs.

In this section, we give a brief review of LSFD for UL signal detection \cite{8845768}. During the UL data transmission, the APs perform local combining on the received signal and generate local estimates of the UL data signal based on their local CSI. The local estimates of UL signal are sent to the CPU via fronthaul links. For the final decoding of the UL data signal, the CPU combines the local signal estimates via LSFD weighting, where LSFD weights are calculated using the LSF coefficients of the channel.

We provide a concise overview of large-scale fading decoding (LSFD) applied to uplink (UL) signal detection as detailed in \cite{8845768}. In the UL data phase, access points (APs) utilize local combining techniques on the incoming signal, creating local UL signal estimates informed by their respective channel state information (CSI). These estimates are then transmitted to the central processing unit (CPU) over the fronthaul. To decode the UL data signal, the CPU employs LSFD, applying weights derived from the channel's large-scale fading (LSF) coefficients.

Fig. \ref{fig-5} validates the effectiveness of the proposed CSIE method for UL LSFD using setups in Cases 1 and 2. For both LSFD-MR and LSFD-LMMSE, the proposed CSIE method achieves nearly the same SE as CSIF. Therefore, the proposed CSIE method can accurately estimate the statistical CSI required for UL LSFD. It is worth noticing that, when LMMSE is adopted as the local combiner at the APs, LSFD with the proposed CSIE requires no CSI exchange between the APs and CPU. 
Fig. \ref{fig-6} validates the effectiveness of the propsoed CSIE methods for DL LSFP under the two setups.  
We can see that, when MR is adopted as local precoder, the LSFP-MR with CSIE deliver similar DL SE for all UEs as LSFP with full CSI feedback. When L-MMSE is adopted for local precoding, the propose LSFP with the proposed statistical channel estimation scheme even slightly outperforms conventional LSFP with full CSI feedback. 
The retional is that the proposed statistical CSI esitmation methods provides a unbiased estimation of the LSF coefficients the effective channel. In contrast, in full CSI feedback, the APs obtain the statistical CSI by averaging $\mathbf{v}^{\mathrm{H}}\hat{\mathbf{h}}_{kl}$, which generates a biased estimation of $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{v}^{\mathrm{H}}\mathbf{h}_{kl}\}$. Consequently, for LSFP-LMMSE, full CSI feedback utilizes the biased estimation of statistical CSI obtained at APs, which leads to performance degradation.

The effectiveness of the proposed channel state information estimation (CSIE) method for uplink (UL) large-scale fading decoding (LSFD) is confirmed in Fig. \ref{fig-5}, using the set configurations from Cases 1 and 2. Both LSFD-MR and LSFD-LMMSE, when coupled with the CSIE method, achieve similar spectral efficiency (SE) to that of the channel state information feedback (CSIF) method. This demonstrates the CSIE method's ability to accurately estimate the statistical CSI essential for UL LSFD. Notably, the use of local LMMSE combining at the APs enables LSFD with the CSIE method to forgo any CSI exchange between the APs and the CPU.

Fig. \ref{fig-6} confirms the efficacy of CSIE for downlink (DL) large-scale fading precoding (LSFP) across both cases. With MR as the local precoder, LSFP-MR employing CSIE nearly matches the DL SE delivered by LSFP with complete CSI feedback for all UEs. When the L-MMSE method is used for local precoding, LSFP equipped with CSIE slightly surpasses the traditional LSFP with full CSI feedback.

The validity of the CSIE methods lies in providing an unbiased estimation of the LSF coefficients of the effective channel. In contrast, full CSI feedback methods typically average $\mathbf{v}^{\mathrm{H}}\hat{\mathbf{h}}_{kl}$, yielding a biased estimate of $\mathbb{E}\{\mathbf{v}^{\mathrm{H}}\mathbf{h}_{kl}\}$. As a result, LSFP-LMMSE experiences performance drop due to the biased statistical CSI estimation used in full CSI feedback scenarios.
